blurb: A Plant Select standout — a flat, reliably evergreen mat of tiny dark green leaves that vanishes under a blaze of cobalt-blue flowers every spring, tough between pavers, on dry slopes, and in any sunny or partly sunny spot where nothing else will grow.
spread: Spreads by creeping stems to form a dense mat; non-invasive; takes light foot traffic
seasons: Reliably evergreen dark green mat year-round; brilliant true-blue flowers Apr–Jun; green mat persists through summer and fall; may show purplish tints in intense sun
wildlife: Early-spring bee forage — flowers are among the first blue blooms of the season when native bees are emerging

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zone 3 — among the most cold-tolerant groundcovers available and well-proven on the Front Range (zones 5b–6a). A Plant Select introduction tested specifically for Colorado conditions; it is fully perennial here. The mat is reliably evergreen, retaining dark green foliage through winter; it may take on attractive purplish tints when exposed to intense summer sun. No winter protection is needed.
care.planting: Plant in spring (April–May) or early fall (August–mid-September) on the Front Range. Spring planting is easiest: nursery plugs or divisions establish quickly in warming soil before summer. Fall planting by early September gives plants time to root before the ground freezes. Space plants 12–15 in. apart; the mat fills in completely within one to two seasons. Turkish speedwell thrives between stepping stones and pavers — set it at the same time you lay the stone, tucking plugs into gaps, or plant along edges and let it creep in. In containers, it forms a neat low carpet but does not trail dramatically; container roots tolerate Front Range winters better than most tender perennials since the plant is fully zone 3 hardy, though sinking pots to the rim or sheltering in an unheated garage over winter is still prudent on exposed patios.
care.sun: Full sun to part sun. Best flowering and the tightest mat come with 6+ hours of direct sun daily; Colorado's intense high-altitude sun suits it well. It tolerates part sun (3–6 hours) but flowering is somewhat reduced and the mat stays lower-growing. Deep shade is not suitable — the plant becomes sparse and blooms poorly.
care.soil: Lean, well-drained to sharply drained soil is ideal — the exact opposite of rich, moist border soil. Sandy, gravelly, or rocky ground is perfect. Tolerates the alkaline clay-loam typical of Boulder yards only if drainage is not impeded; in heavy clay, plant in a raised area, on a slope, or amend the planting pocket with coarse grit. Does not need fertilizer or organic matter at planting.
care.water: Low to xeric. Established plants are among the most drought-tolerant groundcovers for the Front Range, thriving on Front Range rainfall supplemented by occasional deep watering in the driest summer stretches (every 2–3 weeks). Overwatering is a far more common problem than underwatering — wet, poorly drained soil, especially in winter, is the primary cause of failure. Once established, Turkish speedwell needs almost no supplemental irrigation after the first season.
care.spacing: Space plugs or divisions 12–15 in. apart; the mat creeps to fill gaps within one to two seasons. For quick coverage between pavers or on a dry slope, plant at 10–12 in.
care.bloom: April through June on the Front Range — one of the earliest perennial groundcovers to bloom. The mat is covered so densely with tiny cobalt-blue flowers (with a white eye) that the foliage is hidden. Bloom lasts 4–6 weeks; no deadheading is needed or possible at this scale. After bloom the mat remains a tidy, attractive green carpet through summer and fall.
care.feeding: None needed. Lean, unfertilized soil keeps the mat tight and healthy; added nitrogen produces rank, open growth. Do not fertilize.
care.maintenance: Virtually none. Trim any winter-damaged or overly vigorous edges with shears in early spring, just before bloom. No deadheading. The mat is self-sustaining. If it creeps beyond its intended area, pull or cut back the edges in spring or fall.
care.troubles: Very low-maintenance in well-drained, sunny conditions. Crown rot in heavy, poorly drained soil or under excess irrigation is the main failure mode on the Front Range — ensure drainage before planting. No serious pests; deer avoid it. Slugs may nibble in unusually wet conditions.
care.companions: Turkish speedwell is most naturally paired with other xeric, sunny groundcovers and rock-garden plants — creeping thyme, low sedums, hardy ice plant, and ornamental grasses. Excellent between the stones of a dry-laid path or along the front edge of a low-water border where it softens hard edges. Combine with early spring bulbs (species tulips, muscari) whose foliage it covers as they go dormant.
care.propagation: Easiest from division or by detaching rooted stem sections in spring or early fall. Lift a section of established mat, tease apart rooted pieces, and replant 12–15 in. apart; they establish quickly. Cuttings are also reliable: take 2–3 in. stem tips in late spring after bloom (before summer heat), strip the lower leaves, and root in moist gritty sand or perlite in 2–3 weeks. Seed is rarely available for this named selection; vegetative propagation from an established plant is the standard and fastest method.
